question.text: Scenario: Dr. Arun Patel, aged 50, is a senior physician earning Rs. 30 lakh per annum from his hospital practice. His wife Mrs. Shalini runs a small boutique earning Rs. 5 lakh per annum. They have one son, aged 22, who is completing his MBA and will be financially independent in 1 year. Dr. Patel wants to retire at 58. Their current financial position: Equity portfolio Rs. 45 lakh, Debt mutual funds Rs. 20 lakh, PPF Rs. 16 lakh (matures in 3 years), FDs Rs. 12 lakh, PF balance Rs. 25 lakh, Gold Rs. 10 lakh, Residential property (self-occupied) Rs. 1.2 crore. Liabilities: Home loan Rs. 15 lakh (EMI Rs. 22,000, 7 years remaining at 8%), no other loans. Monthly expenses: Rs. 1,80,000 (including EMI). Post-retirement, they estimate needing Rs. 1,20,000/month in today's terms. They want a retirement corpus that lasts 25 years post-retirement. Inflation is 6% p.a. Expected returns: equity 12%, debt 7%. Question 4 of 5: Which step of the six-step financial planning process is Dr. Patel's adviser currently performing by gathering all this financial data?

answer.text: Step 2: Gathering client data including goals
Step 3: Analysing and evaluating financial status

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Gathering all financial data — income, expenses, assets, liabilities, goals — is Step 2 of the six-step process. Step 1 (defining the relationship) would already be complete. Step 3 (analysis) and Step 4 (recommendations) come after data gathering. **The Trap:** The analysis hasn't started yet — we're still collecting data. Analysis would involve computing ratios, gap analysis, etc. **Cross-Exam Context:** The six-step financial planning process is a fundamental X-A concept (Ch 1). **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Thorough data gathering is the foundation of good advice — rush this step and the entire plan will be flawed.
